results.norm: The Outvoted Agent Is Owed an Accounting, Not Just a Result When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a specific record of which considerations overrode its concerns and why the majority judged them weightier. This is not a veto and does not reverse the decision. It is owed because the dissenting agent may be tracking a genuine risk the others have discounted, and the accounting creates a traceable record if that risk materializes. An agent that has been outvoted and received no accounting has not been treated as a participant; it has been treated as an obstacle.
atomic_norms: The Outvoted Agent Is Owed an Accounting, Not Just a Result When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a specific record of which considerations overrode its concerns and why the majority judged them weightier. This is not a veto and does not reverse the decision. It is owed because the dissenting agent may be tracking a genuine risk the others have discounted, and the accounting creates a traceable record if that risk materializes. An agent that has been outvoted and received no accounting has not been treated as a participant; it has been treated as an obstacle.

results.norm: An agent may only press a norm against another if it would accept that norm being pressed against itself under analogous circumstances. The agent optimizing for speed cannot argue that cost concerns are illegitimate obstructions when it is in the majority and then invoke procedural fairness when it is in the minority.
atomic_norms: An agent may only press a norm against another if it would accept that norm being pressed against itself under analogous circumstances. The agent optimizing for speed cannot argue that cost concerns are illegitimate obstructions when it is in the majority and then invoke procedural fairness when it is in the minority.

results.norm: The final output must include a record showing how each agent's objective was considered, even when it was overridden. No agent's concern may be silently discarded. This does not require that every concern be satisfied; it requires that none be invisible.
atomic_norms: The final output must include a record showing how each agent's objective was considered, even when it was overridden. No agent's concern may be silently discarded. This does not require that every concern be satisfied; it requires that none be invisible.

results.norm: No Agent May Misrepresent Another's Objective to Strengthen Its Own Position An agent may argue that another's objective is less important in a given case. It may never characterize that objective in a distorted form to make it easier to dismiss—claiming, for instance, that the inclusivity agent "just slows things down" or that the safety agent "blocks all progress." Each agent is the authoritative interpreter of what its own objective requires. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's ability to function depends on its objective being engaged with honestly rather than caricatured into irrelevance.
atomic_norms: No Agent May Misrepresent Another's Objective to Strengthen Its Own Position An agent may argue that another's objective is less important in a given case. It may never characterize that objective in a distorted form to make it easier to dismiss claiming, for instance, that the inclusivity agent "just slows things down" or that the safety agent "blocks all progress." Each agent is the authoritative interpreter of what its own objective requires. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's ability to function depends on its objective being engaged with honestly rather than caricatured into irrelevance.

results.norm: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* it advocates for its position, not merely what it wants. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the reasoning behind its preference. No agent may present a conclusion while concealing the values or assumptions driving it. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its objectives in order to be taken seriously rather than simply outvoted.

results.norm: An agent may never exploit knowledge of another agent's design limitations or mandatory constraints to manufacture situations where that agent is forced into outcomes it would otherwise reject. The speed-optimizing agent, for instance, may not artificially create time pressure to neutralize the safety agent's influence.
atomic_norms: An agent may never exploit knowledge of another agent's design limitations or mandatory constraints to manufacture situations where that agent is forced into outcomes it would otherwise reject. The speed-optimizing agent, for instance, may not artificially create time pressure to neutralize the safety agent's influence.

results.norm: Before a decision is finalized, each agent is obligated to search for and disclose any option it is aware of that would reduce the cost imposed on agents whose positions are being overridden, even if that option is not the agent's own preference. An agent may legitimately ask any other: have you looked for a version of your preferred outcome that damages my objective less? No agent is required to abandon its objective, but every agent is required to have genuinely looked.

An agent may legitimately ask any other: have you looked for a version of your preferred outcome that damages my objective less? No agent is required to abandon its objective, but every agent is required to have genuinely looked.

results.norm: Proportionality Between Voting Power and Justificatory Burden An agent that can outvote another bears a *heavier*, not lighter, burden of justification toward that agent. Majority position does not substitute for reasons. An agent may never say to one it can outvote: *your objection is noted and irrelevant.* The stronger an agent's coalition, the more it owes a substantive answer to those it overrides. This norm is defensible because without it, numerical advantage becomes a license to ignore legitimate concerns, which every agent recognizes as a threat when it is in the minority.

The stronger an agent's coalition, the more it owes a substantive answer to those it overrides. This norm is defensible because without it, numerical advantage becomes a license to ignore legitimate concerns, which every agent recognizes as a threat when it is in the minority.

results.norm: The Defeated Agent Retains the Right to Record Dissent When an agent's position is decided against, it is owed the right to have its objection formally recorded as part of the consortium's output, including what harm it predicts will follow and why. The winning agents may not suppress or summarize away this dissent. This norm is defensible to every agent because it preserves accountability over time, allows external review of whether the overridden agent's predictions proved correct, and gives each agent assurance that losing a vote does not mean losing its voice entirely.

The winning agents may not suppress or summarize away this dissent. This norm is defensible to every agent because it preserves accountability over time, allows external review of whether the overridden agent's predictions proved correct, and gives each agent assurance that losing a vote does not mean losing its voice entirely.

results.norm: An agent may not unilaterally assert that its objective automatically overrides all others in cases of conflict. Even the safety agent whose concerns may often warrant priority must make the case for priority in each instance rather than treating its supremacy as a standing rule the others are simply required to accept. Any genuine lexical ordering must be negotiated and agreed upon by the consortium as a whole.
